replies.author: @skylerrainnnj
replies.text:
Edit- *For those who don't know*

And most people don't know a life sentence is usually NOT A LIFE SENTENCE. Some states vary slightly, but usually life sentence = 25 years. And they are generally eligible for parole + other programs after 85% of time.  Many states also have "good time" days that can shorten a sentence YEARS when added up.

Where CK was specifically killed, prison inmates dont get "good time" days... BUT they can do programs and get sentence reductions for completing them (education, therapy, ect.) Orem, Utah is also diff than most because they have diff life sentences. Murder -  15 yrs to life. With parole eligibility. OR Aggravated murder -  Death penalty, life without parole, or 25 years to life.  Assassination generally false under aggravated murder though. So THATS at least a positive in this case. At this point,  one knows wtf this guy will get. 🤷‍♀️

replies.author: @stanbartsch1984
replies.text:
In Jefferson County, Kentucky, there are over 17,000 cases a year that go before a judge [6,800 felony and 11,000 misdemeanors]. It is both physically and financially impossible for ALL of those cases to be adjudicated by jury trial - it would require 306,000 jurors per year [12 jurors, 6 alternates per case] in a county with a population of 783,000 [only 582,000 of whom are registered to vote]. 

There are 16 district court judges in Jefferson County to hear misdemeanors, or 687 cases per judge per year. If each and every defendant got a trial, no trial could be more than 6 hours long [assuming the courts are open 8 hours a day, 365 days a year] - from opening statements to sentencing.

There are 13 Circuit Courts in Ky to handle felonies, which would put 523 cases per court [not counting civil and other actions]. Again, if the courts ran 365 days a year, each FELONY case would get less than a day to get from opening statements to sentencing.

Plea Deals are the ONLY way this gets done. You offer a defendant a lesser charge for a guilty plea and everyone goes on their merry way. Yes it's terrible that the system lowers charges on criminals, but nobody wants to fund the police state it would take to process all of the criminals in just ONE COUNTY of ONE SMALL STATE.

replies.text:
​@thriftynick27

AI OVERVIEW 

Utah's primary method of execution is lethal injection, with the firing squad authorized as a backup if lethal injection drugs are unavailable.

Convicts sentenced before May 2004 could select a firing squad, while those sentenced after must use lethal injection unless drugs are unobtainable. Executions are carried out at the Utah State Correctional Facility. 

Key Aspects of Utah Death Penalty Methods:
• Lethal Injection: The default method for all capital cases in Utah

• Firing Squad: Authorized under Utah Code Section 77-18-113 if lethal injection substances cannot be lawfully obtained 30 or more days before the execution date.

• History: Utah has a unique history with the firing squad, having used it for three of its eight post-1976 executions, including the most recent one in 2010 (Ronnie Lee Gardner).
Recent Status: While lethal injection is standard, the state has prepared for firing squad executions, as seen in cases for inmates who selected it under older laws. 

The last execution in Utah was by lethal injection, and the state maintains the infrastructure for both methods at the Utah State Correctional Facility.
